title: ‘Final straw’ for small private hospitals
full_text:
Dozens of private hospitals in regional and rural Australia – including in the marginal Labor seats of Aston, Menzies, and Gilmore – are at risk of losing significant numbers of patients, and some may be “tipped over the edge” if the government manages to abolish the more generous private health insurance rebate for over-65s, according to the sector.
While the Coalition and One Nation are resisting the contentious budget measure, which will save the government $3bn over the forward estimates, to be redirected into aged care, the Greens have offered Anthony Albanese a possible path through parliament.
Greens health spokesman Jordon Steele-John told The Australian the party was open to supporting axing the Howard-era higher rebate for older people.
Amid concerns it could be the “final straw” for many smaller private hospitals, Private Healthcare Australia chief executive Rachel David has written to Health Minister Mark Butler identifying private hospitals in rural and regional Australia and the outer suburbs with more than 70 per cent of patients covered by private health insurance aged over 65. “Prima facie, we consider these hospitals at particular risk,” she writes.
Of the 27 private hospitals identified by Dr David, nine are in Labor electorates and three of those – Aston (Melbourne Eastern Private Hospital) and Menzies (Donvale Rehabilitation Hospital) in Victoria, and Gilmore (Nowra Private Hospital) in NSW – are marginal. The facility with the highest proportion of insured patients aged over 65 is Eden Private Hospital on the Sunshine Coast in Queensland, in Nationals MP Llew O’Brien’s electorate of Wide Bay.
Australian Private Hospitals Association chief executive Brett Heffernan said the “likely exodus” from private health insurance due to the government’s policy would “see hospitals already on the brink of closure tipped over the edge, especially in regional areas”.
“(They’re facing) costs failing to be met by insurers, fuel and delivery costs soaring and no way to recoup those added expenses, nurse wage hikes of up to 35 per cent looming, and now this. It could be the final straw for many,” Mr Heffernan said.
Dr David told The Australian private hospitals serving mostly older people were “at risk of a significant reduction in activity” due to those patients dropping or downgrading their cover next year, with steep price rises estimated – up to $1600 for a couple on a gold policy, according to PHA – if the rebate change takes effect.
“While the government has forecast around 44,000 older people will drop out of health insurance as a result of the proposed change to the rebate, this is a prediction based on confidential assumptions not made public,” Dr David said. “We are also deeply concerned about how many others will downgrade their cover.
“This may push many older people out of private hospitals and on to public hospital waiting lists for procedures they are no longer covered for, such as joint replacements, which are included in gold policies.” The government must convince either the Coalition or the Greens to pass legislation through the Senate, with the lower rebate for over-65s set to start when premiums rise next on April 1.
The Coalition – positioning itself to oppose the measure – has been scathing of the government’s scrapping of the higher rebate for over-65s, with opposition health spokeswoman Anne Ruston rejecting as a “total con” the claim it would fix an intergenerational inequity.
“Cutting the private health rebate for older Australians, many of whom are pensioners, is nothing more than an accounting trick that shifts the cost from the federal budget, but Australian taxpayers still foot the bill,” Senator Ruston said.
“Mark Butler has publicly admitted that for every dollar an older Australian spends on private health insurance, they get back three dollars … So for every older Australian who drops their PHI, the three dollars … previously picked up by the private health insurer will now be picked up by the taxpayer … This measure is a false economy designed to try and cover up Labor’s budget mess.” Senator Steele-John was more favourable. Like the Coalition, the Greens won’t finalise their position until they see the legislation.
“In our view, the government shouldn’t be pitting people against each other. They have a choice, take more from older Australians, or introduce at least at 25 per cent tax on gas corporations,” he said.
Mr Butler said the additional rebate was simply not the best way to spend “precious” taxpayer money on behalf of older Australians when the government needed to do “so much heavy lifting in aged care”.
Search a digital graphic of the hospitals identified as at risk theaustralian.com.
summary: Dozens of small private hospitals in regional Australia face significant patient losses if the government abolishes the higher private health insurance rebate for over-65s. This policy change, aimed at saving $3 billion for aged care, could lead to closures as older patients may drop or downgrade their insurance. The situation is particularly critical for hospitals in marginal Labor electorates, with warnings of increased pressure on public hospital systems.

heading: Health and Disability Insurance
key_points: The government plans to abolish the higher private health insurance rebate for over-65s, risking patient losses for small private hospitals.
Private Healthcare Australia warns that this change could lead to hospital closures, especially in regional areas.
Opposition parties are contesting the measure, citing potential negative impacts on older Australians and public healthcare.
relevance: This issue affects the financial viability of healthcare providers and the broader implications for public health funding, which are critical considerations for actuaries and financial services professionals.
